title: Ionization and Interaction Mechanisms
narrationScript: Everything we do in radiography comes down to a simple question. What happens to a photon when it meets matter? The answer decides how much radiation gets through the part to expose the film, how much scatters and fogs the image, and how much ends up in your body if you stand where you should not. So this lesson is the physics core of the whole method. Start with ionization. An atom is normally neutral. It has as many electrons as protons. If radiation transfers enough energy to knock an electron completely out of the atom, we are left with a free negative electron and a positive ion. Together they are called an ion pair, and creating them is called ionization. In air it takes on average about thirty four electron volts to create one ion pair. If the energy transferred is not enough to remove the electron, the atom can still be excited, meaning an electron is lifted to a higher energy state and then drops back, releasing the energy again. Ionization matters to us for three reasons. It is how the image forms, because film and digital detectors respond to ionization events. It is how we measure radiation, because survey meters and dosimeters collect the charge that ionization liberates. And it is how radiation injures tissue, because ion pairs break the chemical bonds that living cells depend on. One more idea before the mechanisms. X-rays and gamma rays carry no charge and no mass, so they do not ionize steadily along a track the way alpha and beta particles do. They are indirectly ionizing. A photon interacts in a single event, hands its energy to an electron, and that fast electron does nearly all of the actual ionizing as it slows down. Now the interactions themselves. A photon passing through matter either gets through untouched, is absorbed, or is scattered. Three mechanisms cover practically everything we care about in industrial radiography. The photoelectric effect, Compton scattering, and pair production. Which one dominates depends on two things. The photon energy, and the atomic number of the material. The photoelectric effect rules at low energies. The photon strikes a tightly bound inner shell electron and gives up all of its energy in one shot. The photon vanishes. The electron flies off as a photoelectron, and as the vacancy in the inner shell is refilled the atom emits a characteristic X-ray. The chance of a photoelectric event climbs very steeply with atomic number, roughly as the fourth power, and it drops very steeply as photon energy rises, roughly as one over the energy cubed. This steep dependence is why low kilovoltage techniques give such strong subject contrast. Small differences in thickness or density change the absorption a lot. It is also why lead, with its high atomic number, is such an efficient absorber of low energy radiation. Compton scattering takes over through the middle energies, and the middle is where we live. For steel, Compton is the dominant interaction from roughly one hundred kilo electron volts up to about ten mega electron volts, which covers iridium one ninety two, cobalt sixty, and most industrial X-ray work. Here the photon hits a loosely bound outer electron and shares its energy. The electron recoils, and the photon survives, but it leaves in a new direction with less energy. That scattered photon is the troublemaker of radiography. Scatter arriving at the film from the part, the floor, the walls, or the cassette back lowers contrast and fogs the image. Almost everything we do with collimators, masks, filters, and lead screens is a fight against Compton scatter. Compton probability depends mostly on the electron density of the material, not strongly on atomic number, and it declines only slowly as energy rises. Pair production is the high energy mechanism. If a photon carries at least one point zero two two mega electron volts, it can convert into matter as it passes through the strong field near a nucleus. It becomes an electron and a positron. Any extra energy becomes their kinetic energy. The positron does not last long. Once it slows down it meets an electron and the two annihilate, producing two photons of zero point five one one mega electron volts each. Pair production needs energy above the threshold and becomes important only at several mega electron volts, so for ordinary gamma sources it is minor, but it matters in high energy work with linear accelerators and betatrons. Hold on to the big picture. Low energy and high atomic number favor the photoelectric effect, which is where subject contrast comes from. The working middle belongs to Compton scattering, which is where fog and scatter control come from. Very high energy opens the door to pair production. Learn which effect owns which territory, and the rest of radiographic technique starts making sense.
imagePrompt: Inside a concrete exposure vault, a technician in coveralls positions a thick steel step wedge in the collimated beam path of an industrial X-ray tube head while an amber warning beacon glows above the door.

sections.heading: Ionization: The Currency of Radiography
sections.body:
A neutral atom holds as many orbital electrons as it has protons in its nucleus. When radiation transfers enough energy to an orbital electron to tear it completely free, the atom is left with a net positive charge. The freed electron and the positive atom together are an **ion pair**, and the process is **ionization**. If the energy transferred is too small to free the electron, the atom may instead be **excited** — an electron is lifted to a higher energy state and falls back, re-emitting the energy — but no ion pair forms.

Ionization is why radiography works and why radiation is dangerous:

- **Image formation** — film emulsions and digital detectors respond to the ionization produced by radiation that penetrates the part.
- **Detection and measurement** — survey meters, pocket dosimeters, and ion chambers literally collect the charge that ionization liberates in a gas.
- **Biological effect** — ion pairs created in tissue break the chemical bonds living cells depend on, which is the root of every radiation safety rule you follow.

On average it takes about **34 eV** of deposited energy to create one ion pair in air — a number that connects radiation fields to measurable charge, as the next lesson shows.

sections.heading: Photons Interact in Single, Decisive Events
sections.body:
Alpha and beta particles carry charge, so they push and pull on every atom they pass and ionize continuously along a short, dense track. X-rays and gamma rays are different. A photon has **no charge and no rest mass**, so it does not gradually slow down. It travels in a straight line until, in a single event, it interacts — and then it is absorbed outright or scattered into a new direction. Photons are therefore **indirectly ionizing**: each interaction hands energy to an electron, and that fast electron goes on to create nearly all the ion pairs.

Whether any individual photon interacts is pure probability, which is why a beam is attenuated **exponentially** — each layer of material removes the same fraction of the photons entering it, never the same number. Lesson rt1-3-3 builds the shielding math on exactly this fact.

Three interaction mechanisms account for essentially everything that matters in industrial radiography: the **photoelectric effect**, **Compton scattering**, and **pair production**. Which one dominates is decided by two variables — the **photon energy** and the **atomic number (Z)** of the material. Differential absorption between thick and thin, sound metal and discontinuity, is the origin of every image you will ever read.

sections.caption: The three photon interactions that matter in industrial radiography: total absorption, partial scatter, and conversion into an electron-positron pair.

sections.heading: The Photoelectric Effect
sections.body:
In a photoelectric interaction the photon strikes a **tightly bound inner-shell electron** and is **totally absorbed** — the photon ceases to exist. Its entire energy goes to the electron, which is ejected from the atom as a **photoelectron** carrying the photon energy minus the binding energy that held it. The vacancy left in the inner shell is filled by an outer electron dropping inward, and the atom emits the energy difference as a **characteristic X-ray** (or transfers it to another ejected electron).

Two dependencies make this interaction enormously important:

- Probability rises **steeply with atomic number** — roughly as Z⁴.
- Probability falls **steeply with photon energy** — roughly as 1/E³.

The consequences run through all of radiographic technique. At low energies, small differences in thickness, density, or composition produce large differences in absorption — this is why **low-kV techniques give high subject contrast**, and why contrast fades as you turn the kilovoltage up. It is also why **lead** (Z = 82) is such an effective absorber of low-energy photons, both as shielding and as the intensifying screens against your film. In steel the photoelectric effect dominates below roughly 0.1 MeV; in lead its reign extends several times higher.

sections.heading: Compton Scattering
sections.body:
Raise the photon energy and the photoelectric effect fades; **Compton scattering** takes over. The photon now interacts with a **loosely bound outer-shell electron** whose binding energy is negligible by comparison. The photon gives up part of its energy and survives: a **recoil electron** is knocked out of the atom, and a **scattered photon** departs in a new direction with reduced energy. The larger the scattering angle, the more energy the photon loses — a photon scattered straight back has lost the most it can.

Compton probability depends on the **electron density** of the material — nearly the same per gram for all elements — so it follows mass density far more than atomic number, and it declines only slowly with increasing energy. For steel, Compton scattering is the dominant interaction from roughly **0.1 to 10 MeV**, which covers Ir-192 (average ≈ 0.35 MeV), Co-60 (1.17 and 1.33 MeV), and most industrial X-ray work.

Operationally, Compton scattering is both how most image-forming attenuation happens at field energies and **where scatter comes from**: photons scattered in the part, the floor, the walls, the cassette, and the backing arrive at the film from the wrong directions, lowering contrast and fogging the image. Collimation, masking, filtration, lead screens, and the lead letter B backscatter check — tools you will meet later in the course — all exist to manage Compton-scattered photons.

sections.heading: Pair Production
sections.body:
Pair production has a hard entrance fee. A photon passing through the intense electric field near a nucleus can convert from energy into matter, creating an **electron-positron pair**. Each particle has a rest-mass energy of 0.511 MeV, so the photon must carry at least **1.022 MeV** or the interaction is impossible. Any energy above the threshold becomes kinetic energy of the pair. The photon disappears entirely.

The positron does not last. Once it slows, it meets an electron and the two **annihilate**, converting back into energy as **two photons of 0.511 MeV each**, emitted in nearly opposite directions — radiation appearing where there was none before.

Pair production probability rises with energy above the threshold and roughly with **Z²** of the absorber. At Co-60 energies it is barely possible and contributes little. It becomes a major player only at the **multi-MeV energies of betatrons and linear accelerators** used for very thick sections — there, pair production joins Compton scattering as a principal absorption mechanism. For everyday field radiography with Ir-192 or a 300 kV X-ray machine, you can note its existence and move on.

sections.caption: Approximate map of which interaction dominates as a function of photon energy and absorber atomic number, with steel, lead, Ir-192, and Co-60 marked.
The three photon interactions compared.
rows: Possible above 1.022 MeV; significant at several MeV
Follows electron density; weak Z dependence, slow fall with energy
Rises ≈ Z² and with energy above threshold
Subject contrast at low kV; lead's stopping power
Main interaction at field energies; the source of scatter and fog
Megavoltage work (linacs, betatrons); 0.511 MeV annihilation photons

sections.title: Energy and atomic number pick the winner
sections.body: **Low energy + high Z** → photoelectric absorption dominates: total absorption with steep Z⁴ dependence — the source of subject contrast. **Medium energy** (≈ 0.1–10 MeV in steel) → Compton scattering dominates: partial absorption plus the scattered photons that fog film. **Above 1.022 MeV** → pair production becomes possible, significant only at multi-MeV machine energies. Every technique choice — kV selection, source selection, screens, masking — is applied interaction physics.

figures.title: Dominant interaction by energy and atomic number

interactive.title: Which Interaction Is It?
interactive.intro: Sort each statement under the photon interaction it describes.

items.label: The photon transfers all of its energy to an inner-shell electron and disappears
items.why: Total absorption of the photon by a tightly bound electron is the defining signature of the photoelectric effect.

items.label: Dominant interaction in steel at Ir-192 and Co-60 energies
items.why: From roughly 0.1 to 10 MeV in steel, Compton scattering rules — the working range of field radiography.

items.label: Cannot occur below a photon energy of 1.022 MeV
items.why: Creating an electron-positron pair requires two 0.511 MeV rest masses' worth of energy.

items.label: Produces the scattered photons that fog film and demand masking and collimation
items.why: Compton-scattered photons arrive at the detector from wrong directions, cutting contrast and adding fog.

items.label: Probability climbs roughly as the fourth power of atomic number
items.why: The steep Z⁴ dependence of photoelectric absorption is why lead absorbs low-energy photons so well.

items.label: Ends with two 0.511 MeV annihilation photons
items.why: The positron created in pair production annihilates with an electron, converting back into two photons.

items.label: A recoil electron and a lower-energy photon leave the interaction
items.why: Compton scattering shares the photon's energy between an ejected outer electron and a surviving, redirected photon.

items.label: The main reason low-kV techniques produce high subject contrast
items.why: Photoelectric absorption magnifies small thickness and composition differences at low energy.

items.label: Becomes a major absorption mechanism only at multi-MeV accelerator energies
items.why: Pair production needs energy well above its threshold to matter — betatron and linac territory.

checkQuestions.question: During ionization, what is an 'ion pair'?
options: A freed electron plus the positively charged atom it left behind
Two photons emitted in opposite directions
A proton and a neutron ejected from the nucleus
Two atoms sharing an orbital electron
checkQuestions.explanation: Radiation that strips an orbital electron free leaves behind a positive ion; the free electron and positive ion together are the ion pair — the basis of detection, imaging, and biological effect.

checkQuestions.question: In the photoelectric effect, the incident photon:
options: Scatters off an outer electron with reduced energy
Passes through the atom unchanged
Is totally absorbed, ejecting a bound electron from the atom
Converts into an electron-positron pair
checkQuestions.explanation: Photoelectric absorption is all-or-nothing: the photon vanishes and its full energy goes to a tightly bound electron, which is ejected as a photoelectron.

checkQuestions.question: Which interaction dominates in steel across the energies of Ir-192, Co-60, and most industrial X-ray work, and is the principal source of image-fogging scatter?
options: Photoelectric effect
Compton scattering
Pair production
Thermionic emission
checkQuestions.explanation: From roughly 0.1 to 10 MeV in steel, Compton scattering dominates; its scattered photons are the scatter that masking, collimation, and lead screens exist to fight.

checkQuestions.question: What is the minimum photon energy for pair production, and why?
options: 0.511 MeV — one electron rest mass
0.255 MeV — half an electron rest mass
2.044 MeV — four electron rest masses
1.022 MeV — the combined rest-mass energy of the electron-positron pair
checkQuestions.explanation: The photon must supply both 0.511 MeV rest masses, so the threshold is 1.022 MeV; any surplus becomes kinetic energy of the pair.

checkQuestions.question: Subject contrast falls noticeably when a technique is changed from 120 kV to 220 kV on the same steel part. The physics reason is:
options: Pair production begins at 220 kV
Photoelectric absorption, with its steep Z⁴ and 1/E³ dependence, gives way to Compton scattering, which is far less sensitive to thickness and composition differences
The film becomes slower at higher energy
The focal spot grows with kilovoltage
checkQuestions.explanation: Contrast is born in differential absorption. The photoelectric effect magnifies small differences at low energy; as it fades with rising kV, Compton scattering takes over and absorption differences flatten.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics Course §5.1–5.2.3
Standard radiation physics references: photon interaction mechanisms and their Z/energy dependence (general reference)
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2 set the technique variables these interactions explain (general reference)
